in.upgrade_name: Natural Gas Furnace 95% AFUE for All Dwellings with Ducts
restock_description: This upgrade replaces the heating system of applicable dwellings with a high-efficiency natural gas furnace (95% AFUE). Applicable dwellings must have a non-shared, ducted HVAC system that is heated either by electricity (including heat pumps), natural gas (if system is less efficient), 9 propane, or fuel oil (no wood or other fuel).
short_description: Replaces your existing furnace with a high-efficiency natural gas furnace (95% AFUE) that converts more fuel into usable heat, lowering heating costs and improving winter comfort.
long_description: This upgrade replaces an existing natural gas furnace with a high-efficiency condensing furnace rated at approximately 95% Annual Fuel Utilization Efficiency (AFUE). High-efficiency furnaces capture additional heat from exhaust gases that would normally be lost through the flue, allowing them to deliver significantly more heat per unit of fuel. Compared to older furnaces (often 70–80% AFUE), this upgrade can substantially reduce fuel consumption and heating costs. It is most beneficial in homes that already use natural gas heating and have aging or inefficient furnace systems.

in.upgrade_name: Propane Furnace 95% AFUE or Fuel Oil Furnace 88% AFUE
restock_description: This upgrade replaces existing ducted propane and fuel oil furnaces from low efficiency to high efficiency (95% AFUE for propane and 88% AFUE for fuel oil)
short_description: Upgrades your propane or fuel oil furnace to a higher-efficiency unit (95% AFUE propane / 88% AFUE oil), helping reduce fuel consumption and heating costs.
long_description: This upgrade replaces older propane or fuel-oil furnaces with high-efficiency models (approximately 95% AFUE for propane and 88% AFUE for oil systems). These furnaces burn fuel more efficiently and use improved heat exchangers and combustion controls to capture more heat from each unit of fuel. Homes using propane or oil heating often experience meaningful energy savings from this upgrade, particularly when replacing systems installed before modern efficiency standards.

in.upgrade_name: Minimum Efficiency Furnaces and Air Conditioners Circa 2025
restock_description: This upgrade represents a reference case to replace any ducted space heating and cooling equipment at wear-out. It replaces equipment like-for-like with units that meet at least federal minimum efficiency standards as of 2025. This means existing equipment below the efficiency standard are brought up to standard while higher efficiency equipment are replaced with the same equipment (due to wear-out). The federal minimum standard is: - 80% AFUE for natural gas and propane furnaces, - 83% AFUE for fuel oil furnace, - SEER2 13.4 for central AC in southern states, - SEER2 14.3 for northern states, and - SEER2 14.3, 7.5 HSPF2 for ducted air-source heat pump.
short_description: Replaces aging heating and cooling equipment with modern standard-efficiency systems, improving reliability and modestly reducing energy use.
long_description: This upgrade represents a baseline equipment replacement scenario, where outdated heating and air-conditioning systems are replaced with modern systems meeting current minimum efficiency standards. While not as efficient as heat pumps or geothermal systems, these replacements improve reliability and provide modest energy savings by replacing aging equipment with newer, properly sized systems. This option is often considered when systems are nearing end of life and homeowners prefer a conventional replacement rather than switching technologies

in.upgrade_name: Typical Cold Climate Ducted Air Source Heat Pump Circa 2025
restock_description: This upgrade replaces the HVAC system of applicable dwellings with a variable speed ducted airsource heat pump (ASHP) (HSPF2 8.5, SEER2 17.5) with electric resistance backup. Applicable dwellings must have a ducted HVAC system heated by electricity, natural gas, propane or fuel oil (no wood or other fuel).
short_description: Installs an electric cold-climate heat pump that efficiently heats and cools your home, even during cold New Hampshire winters.
long_description: This upgrade replaces the existing HVAC system with a cold-climate air-source heat pump (ccASHP) designed to operate efficiently in low outdoor temperatures. Heat pumps transfer heat rather than generating it, making them significantly more energy efficient than traditional combustion systems. Advanced cold-climate models maintain strong performance at temperatures well below freezing. In addition to heating, the system also provides air conditioning in summer. This upgrade can significantly reduce fossil fuel use and emissions while lowering annual energy costs depending on electricity prices.

in.upgrade_name: Dual Fuel Heat Pump System
restock_description: This upgrade replaces the HVAC system of applicable dwellings with a dual fuel system consisting of an air source heat pump (ASHP) (HSPF2 7.8, SEER2 15.2) and a backup natural gas furnace (92% AFUE if in IECC climate zones 1-4 and 95% AFUE everywhere else). Applicable dwellings must have a ducted, non-shared HVAC system and have access to natural gas.
short_description: Combines an electric heat pump with a high-efficiency gas furnace backup. The system automatically switches between electric and gas heating depending on outdoor temperatures to optimize efficiency and comfort.
long_description: A dual-fuel system integrates an electric air-source heat pump with a high-efficiency gas or propane furnace. The heat pump handles heating during mild and moderate outdoor temperatures when it is most efficient. When temperatures drop very low, the system automatically switches to the furnace for reliable heating. This hybrid approach allows homeowners to reduce fuel consumption and emissions while maintaining strong heating performance during extreme cold conditions.

in.upgrade_name: Single-Speed Geothermal Heat Pump with Thermally Enhanced Grout and Pipes
restock_description: This upgrade replaces the HVAC system of applicable dwellings with a single-speed compressor, variable speed air handler, waterto-air geothermal heat pump (GHP) (3.8 COP, 18.6 EER) with vertical ground heat exchanger. Applicable dwellings must have a ducted HVAC system heated by electricity, natural gas, propane or fuel oil (no wood or other fuel). They must be within a low-rise building with less than 8 stories. Their ground thermal conductivity must be more than 0.8.
short_description: Installs a ground-source (geothermal) heat pump that uses stable underground temperatures for heating and cooling. This significantly improves efficiency compared to conventional HVAC systems and may reduces long-term energy costs.
long_description: This upgrade installs a single-stage geothermal heat pump system connected to underground loops that circulate fluid through the ground. Because underground temperatures remain relatively stable year-round, geothermal systems can transfer heat more efficiently than air-source systems during extreme temperatures. Single-stage systems operate at one heating or cooling output level and are typically simpler and less expensive than multi-stage systems while still delivering significant energy savings compared to traditional heating equipment.

in.upgrade_name: Dual-Speed Geothermal Heat Pump with Thermally Enhanced Grout and Pipes
restock_description:
This upgrade replaces the HVAC system of applicable dwellings with either ducted heating or ducted cooling to a two-speed compressor, variable speed air handler, water-to-air geothermal heat pump (GHP) (4.0 COP, 20.5 EER) with vertical ground heat exchanger. Applicable dwellings must have a
ducted HVAC system heated by electricity, natural gas, propane or fuel oil (no wood or other fuel).
short_description: Installs a ground-source (geothermal) heat pump with two operating stages that adjust heating and cooling output based on demand. By running at a lower stage most of the time and increasing output only when needed, this system operates more efficiently than single-stage equipment.
long_description: Two-stage geothermal heat pumps operate at two levels of heating or cooling output, allowing the system to match the home’s heating demand more closely. During mild conditions the system runs at a lower capacity to improve efficiency and reduce energy use. During colder conditions it operates at full capacity to maintain comfort. Compared to single-stage systems, two-stage geothermal systems typically provide improved efficiency, quieter operation, and more consistent indoor temperatures.

in.upgrade_name: Variable-Speed Geothermal Heat Pump with Thermally Enhanced Grout and Pipes
restock_description: This upgrade replaces the HVAC system of applicable dwellings with a variable-speed compressor, variable-speed air handler, waterto-air geothermal heat pump (GHP) (4.0 COP, 20.5 EER) with vertical ground heat exchanger. Applicable dwellings must have a ducted HVAC system heated by electricity, natural gas, propane or fuel oil (no wood or other fuel).
short_description: Installs a ground-source (geothermal) heat pump with variable-speed operation that continuously adjusts output to match your home’s heating and cooling needs. This provides the highest level of comfort, quieter performance, and maximum energy efficiency compared to single- or two-stage systems.
long_description: Variable-speed geothermal heat pumps represent the most advanced ground-source heating and cooling systems. Instead of operating at fixed output levels, these systems continuously adjust compressor and fan speeds to precisely match the heating or cooling demand of the home. This allows the system to run longer at lower energy levels, which improves efficiency, reduces energy costs, and maintains highly stable indoor temperatures. Variable-speed systems are typically the most efficient geothermal option but also have higher upfront installation costs.

in.upgrade_name: Heat Pump Water Heater
restock_description: This upgrade installs a 240V heat pump water heater (HPWH) to all dwelling units that are not currently using a HPWH or a solar thermal water heater. The equipment has a Uniform Energy Factor (UEF) no less than 3.3 and a First Hour Rating (FHR) no less than 45 gal/h.
short_description: Replaces your existing water heater with a high-efficiency heat pump water heater, which uses electricity much more efficiently to heat water.
long_description: Heat pump water heaters use a refrigeration cycle to extract heat from surrounding air and transfer it to water in the tank. This process can be two to four times more efficient than conventional electric resistance water heaters. Because they move heat instead of generating it directly, they significantly reduce electricity consumption for water heating. These systems are particularly effective when installed in basements or utility rooms with adequate air volume.

in.upgrade_name: High Efficiency Natural Gas Tankless Water Heater
restock_description: This upgrade installs a high efficiency condensing natural gas tankless water heater with a Uniform Energy Factor (UEF) of 0.95 and a burner capacity of 199 kBtu/h to all dwelling units that are not currently using solar thermal, heat pump, or gas tankless water heaters and have access to gas.
short_description: Installs a high-efficiency natural gas water heater that heats water on demand and reduces standby heat losses. Providing you with more reliable hot water.
long_description: This upgrade replaces an existing water heater with a high-efficiency natural gas model, which uses improved burners and heat exchangers to transfer more heat into the water. Compared to older gas water heaters, modern high-efficiency models waste less heat through exhaust gases and standby losses. This upgrade can lower natural gas consumption while maintaining the same hot water capacity and performance.

in.upgrade_name: Air Sealing with Drill and Fill Wall Insulation
restock_description: This upgrade air seals dwellings down to 5 ACH50 if they have an infiltration rate of ≥6 ACH50.
short_description: Seals air leaks in walls, ceilings, and around penetrations to reduce uncontrolled air infiltration. This improves indoor comfort, lowers heating and cooling loads, and can reduce moisture and draft issues.
long_description: Air sealing targets uncontrolled air leakage through cracks, joints, and penetrations in the building envelope, including areas around windows, doors, plumbing penetrations, attic hatches, and electrical outlets. Reducing air leakage helps prevent warm air from escaping in winter and hot air from entering in summer. This improves overall energy efficiency, enhances comfort, and reduces strain on heating and cooling systems of your home.

in.upgrade_name: Attic Floor Insulation for Unfinished Attics
restock_description: This upgrade brings the attic floor insulation in unfinished attics to the prescriptive level from IECC 2021 (R-49 for IECC climate zone 1 and R-60 everywhere else) where the existing insulation is R10 or more less than the
short_description: Increases attic insulation to modern code levels to reduce heat loss through the roof, one of the largest sources of energy waste.
long_description: This upgrade increases insulation levels on the attic floor, which separates the conditioned living space from the unconditioned attic. Heat naturally rises, making the attic one of the most common areas for energy loss in homes. Increasing attic insulation improves the building’s thermal resistance (R-value), reducing heating demand in winter and improving comfort throughout the home.

in.upgrade_name: Duct Sealing and Insulation
restock_description: This upgrade seals and insulates HVAC ducts from more than 6% duct leakage to outside and less than R-6 duct insulation to 5% duct leakage to the outside and R8 duct insulation.
short_description: This upgrade seals and insulates air ducts to reduce energy losses and improve heating and cooling efficiency.
long_description: Many homes lose significant energy through leaky or poorly insulated duct systems located in attics, basements, or crawlspaces. This upgrade seals duct leaks and adds insulation around ducts to prevent conditioned air from escaping before reaching living spaces. Improved duct performance increases HVAC efficiency, reduces energy waste, and can improve airflow and temperature balance throughout the home.

in.upgrade_name: Drill and Fill Wall Insulation with Air Sealing
restock_description: This upgrade applies R-13 drilland-fill insulation to uninsulated exterior wood-stud walls. It also includes env_001 to air seal dwellings down to 5 ACH50 if they have an infiltration rate of ≥6 ACH50. An applicable dwelling may receiv
short_description: This upgrade improves your home's building envelope by sealing air leaks and adding insulation inside exterior walls.
long_description: This upgrade combines air sealing with drill-and-fill wall insulation, a process where insulation is added inside existing wall cavities without major reconstruction. By filling empty or poorly insulated wall cavities and sealing air leaks, the home’s thermal envelope becomes more effective at retaining heat in winter and blocking heat in summer. This can significantly reduce heating demand in older homes with limited wall insulation.

in.upgrade_name: Air Sealing, Attic Floor Insulation, and Duct sealing
restock_description: This upgrade applies air sealing (env_001), attic floor insulation (env_002), and duct sealing and insulation (env_003) to applicable dwelling units. An applicable dwelling may receive some or all individual measures.
short.upgrade_name: Air Sealing + Attic + Duct Insulation Improvements
short_description: A combined upgrade that addresses multiple major sources of energy loss. By upgrading attic insulation, sealing air leaks, and upgrading duct systems. This comprehensive approach improves system efficiency and overall home comfort.
long_description: This package targets multiple major sources of energy loss by combining air sealing, attic insulation improvements, and duct sealing/insulation. Addressing these systems together improves the home’s thermal envelope and HVAC performance simultaneously. Because these upgrades interact with each other, bundled improvements often provide greater overall efficiency gains than implementing each measure individually.

in.upgrade_name: Air Sealing, Attic Floor Insulation, Duct sealing, and Drill-and-Fill Wall Insulation
restock_description: This upgrade applies air sealing (env_001), attic floor insulation (env_002), duct sealing and insulation (env_003), and drilland-fill wall insulation (part of pkg_001) to applicable dwelling units. An applicable dwelling may receive some or all individual measures.
short_description: A whole-jome insulation upgrade that includes air sealing, attic insulation, duct improvements, and wall insulation where applicable. This dramatically reduces heat loss and thus lowers the heating and cooling demand, enhancing long-term energy savings.
long_description: This comprehensive package improves the building envelope by combining air sealing, attic insulation upgrades, duct improvements, and wall insulation. By strengthening the thermal barrier surrounding the home, this upgrade significantly reduces heating demand and improves indoor comfort. Comprehensive insulation improvements are particularly impactful in older homes with poor or inconsistent insulation levels.

in.upgrade_name: ENERGY STAR Windows
restock_description: This upgrade replaces any existing windows with less than the performance level specified by ENERGY STAR Version 7.0 (by climate regions) with windows meeting this performance level. This upgrade includes the air infiltration reduction benefit from window replacement.
short_description: Replaces existing windows with ENERGY STAR–certified models that meet modern thermal performance standards. This reduces heat loss, improves comfort near windows, and lowers HVAC demand.
long_description: This upgrade replaces existing windows with ENERGY STAR–certified high-efficiency windows designed to significantly reduce heat transfer between your home and the outdoors. These windows typically include advanced multi-pane glazing, low-emissivity (Low-E) coatings, and insulated window frames, which work together to reflect heat back into the home during winter and limit unwanted heat gain during summer. By improving the thermal performance of the window system, this upgrade helps reduce heating and cooling demand, leading to lower energy use and improved indoor comfort year-round. High-performance windows can also reduce drafts, improve temperature consistency near windows, limit condensation, and provide better sound insulation from outdoor noise.

in.upgrade_name: Dual-Speed Geothermal Heat Pump with Thermally Enhanced Grout and Pipes, Air Sealing, Attic Floor Insulation, and Duct Sealing
restock_description: This upgrade combines the dualspeed geothermal heat pump (hvac_007) with envelope improvements (pkg_002), which consist of air sealing, attic floor insulation, and duct sealing and insulation.
short_description: This upgrade pairs a two-stage geothermal heat pump system with improvements to the home’s insulation and air sealing. The geothermal system uses stable underground temperatures to provide heating and cooling, while envelope improvements help reduce heat loss and air leakage. Together, these changes can improve overall heating and cooling efficiency.
long_description: This upgrade combines a high-efficiency geothermal heat pump system with key insulation improvements to the home’s building envelope. The geothermal system uses stable underground temperatures to efficiently heat and cool the home, while insulation and air-sealing upgrades reduce the energy required to maintain comfortable indoor temperatures. By improving both the heating system and the building itself, this upgrade maximizes overall energy performance. The result is lower heating and cooling demand, reduced energy costs, and improved indoor comfort throughout the year. Homes that combine efficient HVAC systems with strong insulation typically achieve some of the largest energy savings available through retrofit improvements.

in.upgrade_name: HVAC Demand Flexibility - On-peak Load Shedding, 2F Offset
restock_description: This upgrade curtails loads by setting back the HVAC thermostat setpoints by 2°F throughout a onehour on-peak window.
short_description: This upgrade uses smart thermostat controls to slightly adjust indoor temperatures during peak electricity demand hours. By temporarily shifting the thermostat setpoint by about 2°F, the heating or cooling system runs less when electricity demand is highest, helping reduce peak energy use while maintaining overall comfort.
long_description: This upgrade uses smart thermostat controls to temporarily reduce heating or cooling during peak electricity demand hours. During these periods, the thermostat adjusts the indoor temperature by about 2°F, slightly reducing HVAC operation when electricity demand and grid stress are highest. Because the adjustment is small and occurs only during limited peak periods, most homeowners experience little noticeable change in comfort. By lowering HVAC energy use at critical times of day, this strategy helps reduce electricity costs, decrease strain on the electric grid, and lower overall energy demand while maintaining comfortable indoor conditions.

in.upgrade_name: HVAC Demand Flexibility - Pre-peak Load Shifting, 2F Offset, 4hr Pre-peak Duration
restock_description: This upgrade shifts loads by changing the HVAC thermostat setpoints by 2°F throughout a fourhour pre-peak window to pre-heat or pre-cool the dwelling.
short_description: This upgrade pre-heats or pre-cools the home before peak electricity demand periods by adjusting the thermostat about 2°F up to four hours in advance. By conditioning the home earlier, the heating or cooling system can operate less during peak hours when electricity demand and prices are typically highest.
long_description: This upgrade uses a pre-conditioning strategy that shifts heating or cooling energy use to earlier hours of the day, before peak electricity demand begins. The thermostat adjusts indoor temperatures by about 2°F up to four hours before the peak period, allowing the home’s structure to store thermal energy. This stored heating or cooling helps maintain comfortable indoor temperatures during the peak period while the HVAC system operates less. By shifting energy use away from peak-demand hours, this strategy can reduce electricity costs, lower grid stress, and improve overall energy efficiency without significantly affecting comfort.

in.upgrade_name: HVAC Demand Flexibility - On-peak Load Shedding, 4F Offset
restock_description: This upgrade curtails loads by setting back the HVAC thermostat setpoints by 4°F throughout a onehour on-peak window.
short_description: This upgrade reduces heating or cooling demand during peak electricity periods by temporarily adjusting the thermostat by about 4°F. The larger adjustment lowers HVAC energy use more significantly during high-demand hours while still maintaining acceptable indoor comfort for short periods.
long_description: This upgrade temporarily reduces HVAC operation during peak electricity demand hours by adjusting the thermostat setpoint by approximately 4°F. The larger temperature adjustment results in a greater reduction in heating or cooling demand than smaller load-shedding strategies. While the change in indoor temperature may be slightly more noticeable, it is designed to occur only during limited peak periods to maintain reasonable comfort levels. By lowering HVAC energy use when electricity demand is highest, this strategy helps reduce energy costs, ease pressure on the electric grid, and decrease peak electricity consumption.

in.upgrade_name: HVAC Demand Flexibility - Pre-peak Load Shifting, 4F Offset, 4hr Pre-peak Duration
restock_description: This upgrade shifts loads by changing the HVAC thermostat setpoints by 4°F throughout a fourhour pre-peak window to pre-heat or pre-cool the dwelling.
short_description: This upgrade shifts heating or cooling energy use away from peak demand periods by adjusting the thermostat about 4°F up to four hours before peak hours begin. By conditioning the home earlier, the HVAC system can reduce operation during times when electricity demand and grid stress are highest.
long_description: This upgrade uses a more aggressive preconditioning approach to shift HVAC energy use away from peak-demand periods. The thermostat adjusts indoor temperatures by about 4°F up to four hours before peak electricity demand begins, allowing the home to store additional thermal energy in walls, floors, and furnishings. Because the home has already been pre-heated or pre-cooled, the HVAC system can operate less during the peak demand period itself. This strategy helps reduce peak electricity consumption, lower energy costs, and support grid reliability while maintaining acceptable indoor comfort.

in.upgrade_name: HVAC Demand Flexibility - Pre-peak Load Shifting, 2F Offset, 1hr Pre-peak Duration
restock_description: This upgrade shifts loads by changing the HVAC thermostat setpoints by 2°F throughout a one-hour pre-peak window to pre-heat or pre-cool the dwelling.
short_description: This upgrade uses a short pre-conditioning adjustment to reduce HVAC use during peak electricity demand. The thermostat shifts about 2°F roughly one hour before peak periods, allowing the home to store a small amount of heating or cooling so the system runs less during peak hours.
long_description: This upgrade uses a short-duration pre-conditioning strategy that shifts a portion of HVAC energy use away from peak demand hours. The thermostat adjusts indoor temperatures by approximately 2°F about 1 hour before peak electricity demand, allowing the home to store a small amount of thermal energy in advance. Because the adjustment is both modest and short-lived, this strategy maintains comfort while still helping reduce heating or cooling demand during the peak period. Even small shifts in energy use can help reduce electricity costs and support a more stable and efficient electric grid.
